<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003313 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00121: (2026x Refresh1) Filtering usages

- page_id: `304003313`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003313/2026x+Refresh1+Filtering+usages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:49:21.658+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Resource Usage Map
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

After [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating resource usage maps' space=''], you can use the Filters pane located on the left side of the application portal to choose what types of usages should be displayed in the map. As you can see below, there two filter groups: main filters which you can use in every resource usage map, and filters for suspicious usages which only exist if there are suspicious usages in a map.

To filter resources in a resource usage map, select the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to view and clear the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to hide.

#### TIP: Why a map is empty?

Why a map is empty?

If no usages in your resource usage map meet the criteria of the selected filters, the map is displayed as empty. Select more filters to see resource usages in the map.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### This example of a resource usage map illustrates different types of filters you can use in the Filters pane.

The following tables describes all the filters you can use in a resource usage map:

| Resource usage map filter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Main filters | Main filters are located at the top of the Filters pane. You can use them in every resource usage map to show or hide non-suspicious usages and standard profiles including local used projects. |
| Display non-suspicious usages | Select this filter to show non-suspicious usages in a resource usage map. |
| Display standard profiles | Select this filter to show the usages of standard profiles and local used projects in a resource usage map. |
| Filters for suspicious usages | Filters for suspicious usages are located under the main filters in the Filters pane. You can use them to show or hide different types of suspicious usages. However these filters are displayed only if there are suspicious usages in a resource usage map. |
| Inconsistent version usages | Select this filter to show the resources that use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects. |
| Cyclic usages | Select this filter to show the resources that use themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop. |
| Unused resources | Select this filter to show the resources that are not used by other resources stored in the Teamwork Cloud repository. The Unused resources filter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

As you can see below, there two filter groups: main filters which you can use in every resource usage map, and filters for suspicious usages which only exist if there are suspicious usages in a map.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To filter resources in a resource usage map, select the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to view and clear the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to hide.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3df5ff02-5fc0-4496-9a1e-81651afaec23"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Why a map is empty?</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If no usages in your resource usage map meet the criteria of the selected filters, the map is displayed as empty. Select more filters to see resource usages in the map.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="filters_pane.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Filtering usages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">This example of a resource usage map illustrates different types of filters you can use in the Filters pane.</h6><p>The following tables describes all the filters you can use in a resource usage map:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 76.4125%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.2586%;" /><col style="width: 79.7414%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Resource usage map filter</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Main filters</strong></td><td>Main filters are located at the top of the Filters pane. You can use them in every resource usage map to show or hide non-suspicious usages and standard profiles including local used projects.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Display non-suspicious usages</strong></td><td>Select this filter to show non-suspicious usages in a resource usage map.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Display standard profiles</strong></td><td>Select this filter to show the usages of standard profiles and local used projects in a resource usage map.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Filters for suspicious usages</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>Filters for suspicious usages are located under the main filters in the Filters pane. <span>You can use them to show or hide different types of suspicious usages. </span>However these filters are displayed only if there are suspicious usages in a resource usage map.</span></td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Inconsistent version usages</strong></td><td colspan="1">Select this filter to show the resources that use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Cyclic usages</strong></td><td colspan="1">Select this filter to show the resources that use themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Unused resources</strong></td><td colspan="1">Select this filter to show the resources that are not used by other resources stored in the Teamwork Cloud repository. The <strong>Unused resources</strong> filter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository.</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002333 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00122: (2026x Refresh1) General parameters

- page_id: `304002333`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002333/2026x+Refresh1+General+parameters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:47:29.380+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Deployment Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Configuration > (2026x Refresh1) Authentication server
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following parameters are used with the authentication server to ensure proper working functionality.

| Parameter | Description | Default value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| authentication.token.ttl | The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds. | 900 |
| authentication.code.token.ttl | The authentication code expiration time in seconds. | 15 |
| authentication.implicit.token.ttl | The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds for the implicit authentication flow (e.g., used for MagicDraw UML). | 86400 |
| authentication.unlimited.token.ttl | Authentication ID token that has extremely long expiration time and should only be used in server-server requests when there is a need to perform regular batch jobs. | 630720000 |
| authentication.personal.access.token.ttl | Personal access token that should only be used in server-server requests when there is a need to perform regular batch jobs. | 630720000 |
| authentication.client.ids | The valid client IDs, separated by commas. | MAGICDRAW,webApplicationPlatform,twcSynchronizationManager |
| authentication.client.implicit | The implicit authentication flow client IDs (separated by commas). | MAGICDRAW |
| authentication.client.unlimited | Client IDs that use token ID of unlimited expiration (separated by commas). | twcSynchronizationManager |
| authentication.redirect.uri.whitelist | A list of allowed redirect URIs. The authentication server client should pass a valid redirect URI to an authorized endpoint. | https://${webapp.server.ip}:8443/,https://${twc.server.ip}:8111/,https://md_redirect/ |
| authentication.disable.credentials | Ability to disable username/password authentication if external user authentication (CAC, SAML) is used. | false |
| authentication.saml.login.redirect.enabled | Redirects users directly to the SAML login page. Works only together with authentication.disable.credentials=true and authentication.certificate.enabled=false. | false |
| cassandra.connection.delay | Defines the delay (in seconds) between retry attempts when a connection to Cassandra database is refused. | 30 |
| cassandra.connection.retries | Specifies how many times the application should retry a failed Cassandra database connection. | 12 |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.token.ttl</span></td><td>The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds.</td><td><span>900</span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="17efc81d-2a86-4ecd-a27b-4ff8d34bd359"><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.code.token.ttl</span></td><td>The authentication code expiration time in seconds.</td><td>15</td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.implicit.token.ttl</span></td><td>The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds for the implicit authentication flow (e.g., used for MagicDraw UML).</td><td>86400</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.unlimited.token.ttl</td><td>Authentication ID token that has extremely long expiration time and should only be used in server-server requests when there is a need to perform regular batch jobs.</td><td>630720000</td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">authentication.personal.access.token.ttl</span></td><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Personal access token that should only be used in ````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002262 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00123: (2026x Refresh1) Generating a personal access token

- page_id: `304002262`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002262/2026x+Refresh1+Generating+a+personal+access+token
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:47:24.414+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Admin web applications > (2026x Refresh1) My account application
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A personal access token allows you to authenticate command-line tools without using your username and password.

Always keep your token secure and never share it with anyone you do not trust.

To generate a personal access token

1. Open the My account application.
2. In the Personal access token area, click the Generate Token button.
3. Once the token is generated, click [ATTACHMENT filename='copy.png'] to copy it to your clipboard.

You can use a personal access token in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Used Projects Auto Update Wizard' space='MDTWRT'] with an SSO user.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generating a personal access token.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A personal access token allows you to authenticate command-line tools without using your username and password.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b667c4fc-bed0-48d0-9104-4eb193eb0901"><ac:rich-text-body>Always keep your token secure and never share it with anyone you do not trust.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To generate a personal access token</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the My account application.</li><li>In the <strong>Personal access token</strong> area, click the <strong>Generate Token</strong> button.</li><li>Once the token is generated, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="16"><ri:attachment ri:filename="copy.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generating a personal access token" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to copy it to your clipboard.<br /><br /></li></ol><p><br />You can use a personal access token in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MDTWRT" ri:content-title="Using Used Projects Auto Update Wizard" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Used Projects Auto Update Wizard]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> with an SSO user.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="generating_personal_access_token.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generating a personal access token" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Generating a personal access token.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="20c9d454-4427-4a71-8462-0f4afd0364df"><ac:rich-text-body>To invalidate the personal access token, delete it by clicking <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="delete.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS2024xR2" ri:content-title="Assigning roles" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003338 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00124: (2026x Refresh1) Generating a report

- page_id: `304003338`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003338/2026x+Refresh1+Generating+a+report
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:49:24.581+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Reports application
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To generate a report

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Accessing the Reports application' space='']Open the **Reports** application.
2. Select the desired report template. In this example we use the User details report template: [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select the scope of users. You can select a single, multiple, or all users.
4. Click **Next**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Next, select the format of the report and choose whether to include assigned roles in the reported data: [ATTACHMENT filename='report_options.png']
6. Click Finish and Save to generate and save your report. Below is an example of a User details report file: [ATTACHMENT filename='user_details_report.png']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304001796 space=MCS version=2 -->
## PAGE 00125: (2026x Refresh1) Generating FMU

- page_id: `304001796`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304001796/2026x+Refresh1+Generating+FMU
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-29T12:46:21.962+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Server-side simulation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Prerequisites

INLINE

You can generate an FMU (Functional Mock-up Unit) for external co-simulation of dynamic models that enables the exchange of simulation results between different simulation tools. The generated FMU exposes the defined input and output parameters of a model, as specified in a Simulation Configuration. The generated FMU runs the server-side simulation of a particular Simulation Configuration using given inputs and returns the outputs after each time step. FMUs can be generated for two platforms: Linux 64-bit and Windows 64-bit.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- You must have the same Java HOME version as the modeling tool’s Java version. To check the Java HOME version, run shell in the command prompt. To check the modeling tool’s Java version, go to Help > About > Environment tab.
- You must have a simulation server certificate to co-simulate the generated FMU.
- You must use the generated FMU with tools that use the same JAVA version as the modeling tool; otherwise, you will not be able to run the generated FMU.
- You must add the JAR files to the plugin library to generate FMU. To learn more, refer to .

FMU generation uses selected Simulation Configuration properties, and can contain the following defined data:

- Start Time, End Time, and Step Size property values.
- Input Parameters property value: the default values from the defined input parameters will be included in the generated FMU.
- Output Parameters property value: t he default values from the defined output parameters will be included in the generated FMU.

To generate an FMU

1. Open a Teamwork Cloud project.
2. Right-click a Simulation Configuration and select Simulation > Generate FMU. [ATTACHMENT filename='Generate_FMU_Command.png']
3. In the **Generate FMU** dialog specify the following:
  - In the File Directory box, specify the location where the generated file should be stored.
  - Select the Win64 or Lin64 radio button, depending on the platform on which you will run the generated FMU.
  - Optionally, select the **Include login credentials**check box and specify a server-side simulation server address and login credentials or, token to be stored in the generated FMU file. You can leave the **Include login credentials** check box empty and provide the login credentials or token later as FMU parameters while importing an FMU file. In this case, the credentials will not be stored in the generated FMU file.You can also generate a personal accesstoken instead of a username and password. To learn more about generating a token, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating a personal access token' space='TWCloudTWRT']. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click Generate .

In the case of an FMU file generation error, see the detailed error message in the log file.

##### Adding JAR files to the library

An external library is required to generate the FMU files. You must add the *JAR*files to the plugin library folder.

To add the *JAR*files to the plugin library

1. Download the [ATTACHMENT filename='fmu-builder-2.6.1.jar'] and the [ATTACHMENT filename='fmu-framework-2.6.1.jar'] files.
2. Copy the downloaded JAR files to the <modeling tool installation directory>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\lib path .
3. Restart the modeling tool after copying the *JAR* files.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c896986-882d-423c-bc34-2d572956f754"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can generate an FMU (Functional Mock-up Unit) for external co-simulation of dynamic models that enables the exchange of simulation results between different simulation tools. The generated FMU exposes the defined input and output parameters of a model, as specified in a Simulation Configuration. The generated FMU runs the server-side simulation of a particular Simulation Configuration using given inputs and returns the outputs after each time step. FMUs can be generated for two platforms: Linux 64-bit and Windows 64-bit.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e966c364-7df9-46fa-9614-72a8d454d5a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="b97484a4-6a54-4d9d-ba75-b41b92c93c54">You must have the same Java HOME version as the modeling tool’s Java version. To check the modeling tool’s Java version, go to <strong>Help &gt; About &gt; Environment</strong> tab.</li><li>You must have a simulation server certificate to co-simulate the generated FMU.</li><li>You must use the generated FMU with tools that use the same JAVA version as the modeling tool; otherwise, you will not be able to run the generated FMU.</li><li>You must add the <em>JAR</em> files to the plugin library to generate FMU. In this case, the credentials will not be stored in the generated FMU file.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">You can also generate a personal <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">access<span> </span></span>token instead of a username and password. To learn more about generating a token, refer to </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloudTWRT" ri:content-title="Generating a personal access token" /></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Generate FMU dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li></ul></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7a175496-cc94-432d-85e7-cc7605480050">Click</ac:inline-comment-marker> <strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="0b8177df-46a2-4a1d-9f4f-7b709cc7c4e5">Generate</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong>.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9aaf3281-df90-4fb0-987d-07749a422358"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the case of an FMU file generation error, see the detailed error message in the log file.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Adding JAR files to the library</h3><p>An external library is required to generate the FMU files. You must add the <em>JAR </em>files to the plugin library folder.</p><p>To add the <em>JAR </em>files to the plugin library</p><hr /><ol><li>Download the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="fmu-builder-2.6.1.jar" /></ac:link><em> </em>and the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="fmu-framework-2.6.1.jar" /></ac:link> files.</li><li>Copy the downloaded <em>JAR </em>files to the <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\lib </em>path<em>.</em></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Restart the modeling tool after copying the <em>JAR</em> files.</span></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003256 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00126: (2026x Refresh1) Generating resource revisions report

- page_id: `304003256`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003256/2026x+Refresh1+Generating+resource+revisions+report
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:48:46.075+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Resources application
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can export the history of any Teamwork Cloud project into an Excel spreadsheet. Sharing project revision reports can be a quick and easy way to show progress to team leads or project managers.

To generate and download the resource revisions report

1. Navigate to the Resources application and go to the category where the relevant resource is located.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] next to the resource and select Generate revisions report.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and editing resource details' space='']Open the **Resource** pane. On the resource details card click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select Generate revisions report .
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and editing resource details' space='']Open the **Resource** pane . On the top right of the History card click the icon displayed below: [ATTACHMENT filename='generate_revisions_report_icon_on_history_card.png']
3. In the window that opens, select Download to generate and download the report.

Below is an example of a resource revisions report in XSLS format:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Resource revisions report.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

On the resource details card click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generating resource revisions report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and </span>select<strong> Generate revisions report</strong><span>.</span></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Viewing and editing resource details" /><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Resource</strong> pane</ac:link-body></ac:link>. On the top right of the <strong>History</strong> card click the icon displayed below:<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="generate_revisions_report_icon_on_history_card.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generating resource revisions report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>In the window that opens, select<strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> Download </strong>to generate and download the report.</li></ol><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Below is an example of a resource revisions report in XSLS format:</span></p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Resource revisions report" ac:alt="Resource revisions report"><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_revisions_report.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generating resource revisions report" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Resource revisions report.</span></h6>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003091 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00127: (2026x Refresh1) Getting document information

- page_id: `304003091`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003091/2026x+Refresh1+Getting+document+information
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:48:29.850+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > (2026x Refresh1) Structure of the document portal
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Each Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document contains detailed document and project information. The following steps outline how to access this information.

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document information

1. Open a Cameo Collaborator document.
2. In the app bar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] and select Document information .

The document information screen opens, displaying the following information:

- Document information:
  - Document creation time
  - The name of the user who created the document
  - The last time the document was published
  - The name of the user who published the latest document version
  - The number of the latest document version
  - Indication if commenting and/or editing is enabled and the template that was used to publish the document
- Project information:
  - Project creation time
  - The name of the user who created the project
  - The last time the project was modified
  - The name of the last user to modify the project
  - The number of the latest project version
  - Project branch name

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The example of the Cameo Collaborator document information screen.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Each Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document contains detailed document and project information. The following steps outline how to access this information.</p><p><br /></p><p>Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document information</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a Cameo Collaborator document.</li><li>In the app bar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting document information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and select <strong>Document information</strong>.</li></ol><p><br />The document information screen opens, displaying the following information:</p><ul><li>Document information:<ul><li>Document creation time</li><li>The name of the user who created the document</li><li>The last time the document was published</li><li>The name of the user who published the latest document version</li><li>The number of the latest document version</li><li>Indication if commenting and/or editing is enabled and the template that was used to publish the document</li></ul></li><li>Project information:<ul><li>Project creation time</li><li>The name of the user who created the project</li><li>The last time the project was modified</li><li>The name of the last user to modify the project</li><li>The number of the latest project version</li><li>Project branch name</li></ul></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="document_information.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting document information" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The example of the Cameo Collaborator document information screen.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003024 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00128: (2026x Refresh1) Getting resource links

- page_id: `304003024`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003024/2026x+Refresh1+Getting+resource+links
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:48:24.523+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) User Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Resources application > (2026x Refresh1) Working with resources
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you want to share a Teamwork Cloud project or Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link, you can get it as described below.

To get a Teamwork Cloud project link

1. Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] next to the project, and select Get project link .
3. When the window with the project link opens, click Copy .

Once you complete the steps above, the link to the project is copied to your clipboard.

To get a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link

1. Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] next to the project, and do one of the following:
  - Select Get resource link to if you want to get the link for registered users.
  - Select Get public resource link if you want to share the link publicly.
3. When the window with the project link opens, click Copy .

Once you complete the steps above, the document link is copied to your clipboard and you can share it with reviewers.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you want to share a Teamwork Cloud project or Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link, you can get it as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To get a Teamwork Cloud project link</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting resource links" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> next to the project, and select <strong>Get project link</strong>.</li><li>When the window with the project link opens, click <strong>Copy</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>Once you complete the steps above, the link to the project is copied to your clipboard.</p><p><br /></p><p>To get a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Getting resource links" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> next to the project, and do one of the following:<ul><li>Select <strong>Get resource link</strong> to if you want to get the link for registered users.</li><li>Select <strong>Get public resource link</strong> if you want to share the link publicly.</li></ul></li><li>When the window with the project link opens, click <strong>Copy</strong>.</li></ol><p><br />Once you complete the steps above, the document link is copied to your clipboard and you can share it with reviewers.</p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304003077 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00129: (2026x Refresh1) Global element usage indexing and search

- page_id: `304003077`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304003077/2026x+Refresh1+Global+element+usage+indexing+and+search
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:48:27.904+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Administration Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Product administration
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can index resources for their element usages within the selected scope of the Teamwork Cloud repository and later query these usages through the modeling tool. This enables you to evaluate what impact modifying or deleting certain model elements will have on other models that are using it. To enable this functionality, Teamwork Cloud needs to be incorporated with a dedicated search engine - Elasticsearch. This component makes it possible to index model element usage data and serve it quickly when queried from a modeling tool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Conceptual solution for the global element usage indexing and search functionality.

To start using the global element usage search functionality, you need to install and set up Elasticsearch (v7 series) as described below.

##### Installing and setting up Elasticsearch

From 2026x Hot Fix 2 onwards, Teamwork Cloud uses Elasticsearch version 8 and will not work with older deployments out of the box. 
Therefore, a rolling upgrade needs to be done for the Elasticsearch cluster. Referring to the following guide, first upgrade the cluster to version 7.17.0, then upgrade the cluster to 8.19.0: 
[https://www.elastic.co/docs/deploy-manage/upgrade/deployment-or-cluster/elasticsearch](https://www.elastic.co/docs/deploy-manage/upgrade/deployment-or-cluster/elasticsearch)

###### System requirements

- We recommend using NVMe SSD disks for the Elastic search instance.
- The **-Xmx** value should be set to 4GB as a minimum.
- The expected indexing speed under recommended hardware settings is roughly 1 revision per second.
- The following formula can be used to roughly determine the required disk space per single Indexing Configuration:

*Used Project size (element count) x 400 bytes x N x # of Using Projects (specified scope)*,

where N equals the number of times both using and used elements get changed throughout their history (N tends to range from 4 to 10 as noted from experiments with production data). 
The formula also assumes that actual element-level reference ratio is 15-20% from all of the elements in a Used Project. 
E.g. A DB size of 250GBs yields an index size of 60GBs under the above mentioned conditions.

###### Installing Elasticsearch

Go to [https://www.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch](https://www.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch) and install Elasticsearch (version 8, preferably 8.19.0).

#### NOTE: Important

Important

- We highly recommend deploying Elasticsearch on a machine separate from Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra due to additional server resource consumption.
- In the Elasticsearch installation directory, open the **jvm.options** file and make sure the **-Xms** and **-Xmx** properties are uncommented.
- In setups where encryption is not used, the*elasticsearch.yml*needs to be configured to disable it (enabled by default starting with version 8):

```shell

```

Enabling global element usage indexing and search

Once you install Elasticsearch, configure Teamwork Cloud as described below to start using the global element usage search functionality.

To enable global element usage search

1. Go to *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration*, open the **application.conf** file, and add the following lines at the end of the file:
  1. To enable the querying component, add the following property and set it to *true*:
  2. To enable resource indexing, add the following property and set it to *true*:
  3. If Elasticsearch and Teamwork Cloud run on different machines, add the following line: "]]>
  4. If Elasticsearch is set up to listen on a different port from the default one (9200), add the following line: ]]>
2. Restart Teamwork Cloud services.

Now you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Creating indexing configurations' space=''] in the Settings application and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Global element usage search' space='MDTWRT'] in a modeling tool.

###### Enabling Encrypted Communication (SSL) with ElasticSearch Server

SSL setup with Elasticsearch is a general security feature, required both to configure Elasticsearch itself and Teamwork Cloud. You must specify a username and password. See more information on setting up passwords in Elasticsearch:

https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/7.16/security-minimal-setup.html

To enable encrypted communication between Teamwork Cloud and ElasticSearch servers

1. Prepare type PCKS #12 (*.p12) certificate. You can:

- 
  - Use an existing certificate for the machine that will host Elasticsearch, or
  - Generate a new certificate for Elasticsearch using elasticsearch-certutil ( https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/certutil.html ).

Example:

```text
]]>
```

Alternatively, if the certificate subject name does not match the hostname, you can generate the certificate to facilitate the **Subject Alternative Name**extension:

```text
--dns  --ip ]]>
```

2. Configure *elasticsearch.yml.*

Enable security:

```text

```

Enable SSL for transport:

```text
xpack.security.transport.ssl.keystore.password: changeit]]>
```

Enable SSL for HTTP (REST API):

```text
xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.password: changeit]]>
```

3. Import the certificate into Java's trusted certificates keystore*cacerts*.

First, export the X.509 certificate (usually *.crt or *.cer file) from the *.p12 file (for this use tools like *OpenSSL*or*KeyStore Explorer*). Then import the certificate file using Java's keytool:

```text
-cacerts -alias ]]>
```

4. Set up Teamwork Cloud: in application.conc, set the following properties:

```text
"
esi.query.es.node.protocol = "https"]]>
```

###### Pointing multiple Teamwork Cloud instances to one Elasticsearch instance

Instead of using a separate Elasticsearch instance for each Teamwork Cloud instance, you canpoint multipleTeamwork Cloud instances or clusters toa single Elasticsearch instance.

To point multiple Teamwork Cloud instances to one Elasticsearch instance

1. Go to *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration*, open the **application.conf** file.
2. Uncomment the following setting and specify a valid suffix. Note that each Teamwork Cloud instance pointing to the same Elasticsearch instance needs a unique suffix:

```text

```

ac:macro-id="b5b1e630-fa85-4e30-a0e0-426c83a1dcd8" ac:name="code" style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">To enable resource indexing, add the following property and set it to <em>true</em>:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="81f02ab8-6e99-4a00-b904-65f77c3a8786" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.indexer.enabled = true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">If Elasticsearch and Teamwork Cloud run on different machines, add the following line:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="52486740-8419-4838-9a3d-59d681c99c28" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.node.host = "<es.host.or.ip>"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">If Elasticsearch is set up to listen on a different port from the default one (9200), add the following line:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="4c056d48-30ce-47af-8dfb-a37e7b682a4f" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.node.port = <es.port>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li>Restart Teamwork Cloud services.</li></ol><p style="text-align: left;"><br />Now you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Creating indexing configurations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[index resources for their element usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the Settings application and use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Global element usage search" ri:space-key="MDTWRT" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[global element usage search functionality]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in a modeling tool.</p><h4 style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Enabling Encrypted Communication (SSL) with ElasticSearch Server</span></h4><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="272e5fe1-6f54-4172-9a2e-ba86d49c5dee" ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:rich-text-body><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">SSL setup with Elasticsearch is a general security feature, required both to configure Elasticsearch itself and Teamwork Cloud. You must specify a username and password. See more information on setting up passwords in Elasticsearch: </span><a href="https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/7.16/security-minimal-setup.html" style="text-align: left;">https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/7.16/security-minimal-setup.html</a></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To enable encrypted communication between Teamwork Cloud and ElasticSearch servers</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Prepare type PCKS #12 (*.p12) certificate. You can:</li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Use an existing certificate for the machine that will host Elasticsearch, or</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Generate a new certificate for Elasticsearch using elasticsearch-certutil </span>(<a href="https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/certutil.html">https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/certutil.html</a>).</li></ul></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Example:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="383d5e01-1327-47d9-9b6f-a4d0361393a9" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[elasticsearch-certutil cert --self-signed --name <elasticsearch.host.name>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Alternatively, if the certificate subject name does not match the hostname, you can generate the certificate to facilitate the <strong>Subject Alternative Name </strong>extension:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="08d3ed99-e33e-40ae-92ce-7eda5a2655b6" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[lasticsearch-certutil cert --self-signed --name <arbitrary.subject.name> --dns <elasticsearch.host.name> --ip <elasticsearch.host.ip>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>       2. Configure <em>elasticsearch.yml.</em></p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Enable security:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="2c1350f6-ff3c-4f0f-bf87-f4528932950e" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[xpack.security.enabled: true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Enable SSL for transport:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="80941c84-1392-4aa1-ab2b-3d8a7f51523f" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
xpack.security.transport.ssl.keystore.path: <path.to.p12.file>
xpack.security.transport.ssl.keystore.password: changeit]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Enable SSL for HTTP (REST API):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="056348e0-41b5-4600-a3d2-7f00cecba3dc" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
xpack.security.http.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.path: <path.to.p12.file>
xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.password: changeit]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;">       3. Import <span>the certificate into Java's trusted certificates keystore </span><em>cacerts</em><span>.</span></p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;text-align: left;">First, export the X.509 certificate (usually *.crt or *.cer file) from the *.p12 file (for this use tools like <em>OpenSSL </em><span>or </span><em>KeyStore Explorer</em><span>). Then import the certificate file using Java's keytool:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="ca2a4510-4731-465c-98d2-64b0a04b4969" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[keytool.exe -import -file <path.to.certificate.file> -cacerts -alias <certificate.subject>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">       4. Set up Teamwork Cloud: i</span>n application.conc, set the following properties:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="5039d3e9-0172-4f6b-9606-69628e27aa53" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.security.enabled = true
esi.query.es.security.auth.username = "elastic"
esi.query.es.security.auth.password = "<elastic.password>"
esi.query.es.node.protocol = "https"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h4><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Pointing multiple Teamwork Cloud instances to one Elasticsearch instance</span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Instead of using a separate <span>Elasticsearch instance for each Teamwork Cloud instance, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="82684633-a075-4058-b81b-47f51c8b016b">you can </ac:inline-comment-marker></span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="82684633-a075-4058-b81b-47f51c8b016b">point <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span>multiple </span>Teamwork Cloud instances or clusters to </span>a single Elasticsearch instance</ac:inline-comment-marker></span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="82684633-a075-4058-b81b-47f51c8b016b">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bfdba542-ccf7-440e-9751-8e263d909b7d">To point multiple Teamwork Cloud instances to one Elasticsearch instance</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Go to <em><span>&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration</span></em>, open the <strong>application.conf</strong> file.</span></li><li data-uuid="ac96f46f-42c0-4304-b3ce-8615323a17af"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c0bc5bff-8662-4538-b06c-834c5702f9a8">Uncomment the following setting and specify a valid suffix. Note that <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">each Teamwork Cloud instance pointing to the same Elasticsearch instance needs a unique suffix:</span></ac:inline-comment-marker></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:macro-id="d1c6cd75-3927-483b-87d9-0819ed283df7" ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# Custom suffix for Elasticsearch index names. Allows for separating indexes of separate TWCloud instances/clusters
# if they are using same Elasticsearch instance. Uncomment and enter a non-empty alphanumeric value to enable.
# NOTE: make sure to set the same suffix for all nodes of the same TWCloud cluster!
#
# esi.query.es.index-name-suffix = "CHANGE_ME"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304001991 space=MCS version=2 -->
## PAGE 00130: (2026x Refresh1) Hardening Cassandra

- page_id: `304001991`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304001991/2026x+Refresh1+Hardening+Cassandra
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:53:50.912+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Deployment Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Configuration > (2026x Refresh1) Hardening the deployment and its services
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Used scripts**

Download the *harden_cassandra_ports.sh* script [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html).

##### Cassandra Authentication

By default, Cassandra is deployed with the AllowAllAuthenticator. This authenticator, as the name implies, is an anonymous authenticator which performs no checks.

If you require authenticated connections, you will need to make changes to your **cassandra.yaml** and change the authenticator to PasswordAuthenticator. If you are running a multi-node Cassandra cluster, you need to change the replication factor of the system_auth keyspace.

Detailed instructions can be found at [https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html](https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html)

After making the changes to Cassandra, you will need to update the Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudconfigurations to utilize authentication.

The required changes are as follows:

```text

```

Where *newcassandrauser* and *newcassandrapassword* correspond to the user and credentials which you configured in Cassandra.

###### SSL authentication for Cassandra

If the Cassandra certificate is**not signed by a public certificate authority**, import the Cassandra public certificateto the product's Java cacertsby running the following command:

```bash

```

**Java cacerts default location****for the product**(example):*/etc/alternatives/jre_17/lib/security/cacerts*

##### Cassandra Encrypted Connections

Starting from Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudversion 2021x, encrypted communication with Cassandra is supported. To achieve this, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Deployment configuration' space=''] is needed.

##### Data Encryption at Rest

Open source Cassandra does not support encryption at rest. If you require encryption at rest, you need to use a disk encryption layer.

##### Configuration file property encryption

Apache Cassandra (open source version) does not support encrypting cassandra.yaml properties values.

##### Port access for the firewall

When installing on Linux using our deployment scripts, all of the ports required by Cassandra for inter-node communication, as well as for the Teamwork Cloud nodes to communicate with Cassandra nodes, are opened globally. This configuration is deployed mostly to facilitate testing of the environment upon installation, prior to taking any measures to harden the installation. If we check the firewall upon installation, we will see an output similar to the one below:

```text

```

In our deployment, we create a firewall service definition to facilitate the management of the rules. This file is located in */etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml*, and contains the following:

```text
cassandra
    cassandra
    
    
        
        
        


]]>
```

The first step in securing Cassandra is to limit traffic only to Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud nodes. In the example below, we have a single-node Cassandra/product installation, with its IP address set to 10.254.254.56.

As can be seen below, the firewall configuration has been modified to only allow access from itself.

```text

```

The process to follow is to remove the general firewall allowance to the Cassandra service. After that, we want to ensure that if direct port rule assignments were made, they are removed. Finally, we want to create a set of rich rules that will allow access only to the required nodes.

If your deployment consists of a single-node or multi-node cluster where both Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudand Cassandra reside on the same nodes, automate this process by [downloading](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/(2026x Refresh1) Downloadable+scripts)and using the *harden_cassandra_ports.sh* script. The script is structured in such a way that it will output the rich rule commands to the screen to facilitate copying and modifying to add nodes, which is needed if you have Teamwork Cloud nodes that are not part of the Cassandra cluster.

The *harden_cassandra_ports.sh* script should be executed on all nodes of a multi-node Cassandra cluster, with all nodes being in an operational state.

For Windows users: Create firewall rules restricting access on the aforementioned ports only to authorized nodes - Cassandra nodes and Teamwork Cloud nodes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Used scripts</strong></p><p>Download the <em>harden_cassandra_ports.sh</em> script <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html">here</a>.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Cassandra Authentication</span></h3><p>By default, Cassandra is deployed with the AllowAllAuthenticator. This authenticator, as the name implies, is an anonymous authenticator which performs no checks.</p><p>If you require authenticated connections, you will need to make changes to your <strong>cassandra.yaml</strong> and change the authenticator to PasswordAuthenticator. If you are running a multi-node Cassandra cluster, you need to change the replication factor of the system_auth keyspace.</p><p>Detailed instructions can be found at <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html">https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html</a></p><p>After making the changes to Cassandra, you will need to update the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>configurations to utilize authentication.</p><p>The required changes are as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6bda044e-e33a-4315-a378-87de036a7130"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[auth-provider {
     # Enable this section to enable the cassandra authentication.
     class = PlainTextAuthProvider
     username = newcassandrauser
     password = newcassandrapassword
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where <em>newcassandrauser</em> and <em>newcassandrapassword</em> correspond to the user and credentials which you configured in Cassandra.</p><h4>SSL authentication for Cassandra</h4><p><span>If the Cassandra certificate is</span><span> </span><strong>not signed by a public certificate authority</strong><span>, import the Cassandra public certificate </span><span>to the product's Java cacerts </span><span>by running the following command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9fea3b67-aa63-4e76-a75a-1878c4bb8957"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[keytool.exe -import -trustcacerts -keystore cacerts -storepass changeit -noprompt -alias 'server-alias' -file server.cer]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Java cacerts default location </strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><strong>for the product </strong>(example</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">): </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">/etc/alternatives/jre_17/lib/security/cacerts</em></p><h3>Cassandra Encrypted Connections</h3><p>Starting from <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>version 2021x, encrypted communication with Cassandra is supported. To achiev<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="98bf404f-b92c-4795-b027-182f684c920f">e this,</ac:inline-comment-marker> the <ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring secure SSL communication between Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Deployment configuration" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[configuration of secure SSL communication]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> is needed.</p><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="67214981-c66f-453e-840f-ecc541affddf">Data Encryption at Rest</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>Open source Cassandra does not support encryption at rest. If you require encryption at rest, you need to use a disk encryption layer.</p><h3>Configuration file property encryption</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Apache Cassandra (open source version) does not support encrypting cassandra.yaml properties values.</span></p><h3>Port access for the firewall</h3><p>When installing on Linux using our deployment scripts, all of the ports required by Cassandra for inter-node communication, as well as for the Teamwork Cloud nodes to communicate with Cassandra nodes, are opened globally. This configuration is deployed mostly to facilitate testing of the environment upon installation, prior to taking any measures to harden the installation. If we check the firewall upon installation, we will see an output similar to the one below:</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bd77a12c-ab8a-4d19-913f-8c564b224951"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# firewall-cmd --list-all
public (active)
  target: default
  icmp-block-inversion: no
  interfaces: eth0
  sources:
  services: cassandra lmadmin ssh twcloud
  ports:
  protocols:
  masquerade: no
  forward-ports:
  source-ports:
  icmp-blocks:
  rich rules:


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In our deployment, we create a firewall service definition to facilitate the management of the rules. This file is located in <em>/etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml</em>, and contains the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d4e3771-a365-4d1b-9eff-877db6449eae"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# cat /etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<service version="1.0">
    <short>cassandra</short>
    <description>cassandra</description>
    <port port="7000" protocol="tcp"/>
    <port port="7001" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9042" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9160" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9142" protocol="tcp"/>
</service>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The first step in securing Cassandra is to limit traffic only to Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud nodes. In the example below, we have a single-node Cassandra/product installation, with its IP address set to 10.254.254.56.</p><p>As can be seen below, the firewall configuration has been modified to only allow access from itself.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f056107c-d95f-4d93-870b-dc3a8af27d77"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# firewall-cmd --list-all
public (active)
  target: default
  icmp-block-inversion: no
  interfaces: eth0
  sources:
  services: lmadmin ssh twcloud
  ports:
  protocols:
  masquerade: no
  forward-ports:
  source-ports:
  icmp-blocks:
  rich rules:
        rule family="ipv4" source address="10.254.254.56" service name="cassandra" accept

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The process to follow is to remove the general firewall allowance to the Cassandra service. After that, we want to ensure that if direct port rule assignments were made, they are removed.  Finally, we want to create a set of rich rules that will allow access only to the required nodes.</p><p>If your deployment consists of a single-node or multi-node cluster where both <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>and Cassandra reside on the same nodes, automate this process by <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/(2026x Refresh1) Downloadable+scripts"> downloading </a>and using the <em>harden_cassandra_ports.sh</em> script. The script is structured in such a way that it will output the rich rule commands to the screen to facilitate copying and modifying to add nodes, which is needed if you have Teamwork Cloud nodes that are not part of the Cassandra cluster.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b5c56b3-de21-478c-9c44-2dc920964c70"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <em>harden_cassandra_ports.sh</em> script should be executed on all nodes of a multi-node Cassandra cluster, with all nodes being in an operational state.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="327ecc3e-fa51-4949-b474-aef3d8c51f32"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For Windows users: Create firewall rules restricting access on the aforementioned ports only to authorized nodes - Cassandra nodes and Teamwork Cloud nodes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304002039 space=MCS version=2 -->
## PAGE 00131: (2026x Refresh1) Hardening the deployment

- page_id: `304002039`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/304002039/2026x+Refresh1+Hardening+the+deployment
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-04-29T10:51:51.640+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x Refresh1) Deployment Guide > (2026x Refresh1) Configuration > (2026x Refresh1) Hardening the deployment and its services
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Used files**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='twc.java.security']

##### Protocols and Ciphers

falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcconsists of Java-based services - Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) and WebApp (webapp).

Teamwork Cloud requires a supported Java version (see [System requirements](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=system-requirements&version=2026x Refresh1)) (Java location varies depending on how it was deployed and configured), whereas Web Application Platform uses a bundled Java, located in*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform**/jre/*.

Therefore, in order to harden these services, we must begin by hardening the JVM. The default settings for the JVM are located in **java.security**.

#### EXPAND: How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port

How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port

We can check the ciphers/protocols being used by the applications using nmap (version 7.x) or TestSSLServer.jar, available from [https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x](https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x).

As an example, below is a scan using both tools against a default installation. In this example, we will be testing port 8111, the Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloudport.

```text

```

As can be observed above, the default configuration using OpenJDK 1.8.0_242 allows TLS v1.0 and v1.1, which are deprecated. Additionally, we can see that several key exchanges are taking place using dh1024.

We then proceed to harden the configuration.

Since we are dealing with ciphers, you need to make sure that you do not disable a cipher required by your certificates.

After hardening the VM, we end up with a different set of allowed ciphers and protocols, as shown below.

```text

```

The process of hardening the JVM requires making some changes to the **java.security** file. While these can be made directly, the downside is that if you upgrade your JVM, you will have to reapply your changes.

However, we can place our modifications in our own file, and simply pass a parameter to the JVM upon invocation so that it will apply our changes.

For example, we can create a file *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password*, and pass a parameter to the JVM in the form of *-Djava.security.properties=*<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security*.*

Our hardened security settings are as shown below:

```text

```

To apply these settings, we need to make changes to both the Teamwork Cloud and Web App Platform services.

1. **Teamwork Cloud service** 
For Linux, edit*<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options*and add a line as shown below: jvm.options/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security]]> For Windows, edit the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options* and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.
2. **Web App Platform service** 
For Linux,*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/bin/setenv.sh*and add the directive to the**JVM_OPTS**variable as shown below: setenv.sh/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security"]]> On Windows, you need to edit the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\WebApp\Parameters\Java\Options* and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.

JMX

By default, the Teamwork Cloud service activates a JMX remote port to facilitate application monitoring. The default configuration does not contain any form of authentication.

On Linux, the configuration is located in *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options*.

On Windows, it is located in the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options*.

```text

```

These settings can be removed, thereby removing JMX remote access.

If you would like to allow remote JMX access but require authentication, you can do so by adding settings. For complete documentation, please refer to the Java documentation.

As an example, the below configuration adds password authentication:

```text
/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.access.file=/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.access
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false]]>
```

As can be seen, we are pointing to a set of files (*<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/jmx.password* and *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/twc.java.security*) that control who can access these files.

The vulnerability vector is one whereby JMX could be exploited to execute code. To prevent this, we allow only an authenticated user (jmx.password) who has read-only rights (jmx.access).

```text

```

```text

```

In this example, we created a user (monitoring) with a password (DqzbksT4ET), who can only read values via Remote JMX, but cannot write or execute anything via JMX.

The password and access files have a very stringent ownership requirement. They need to be owned by the user running the process and be accessible exclusively to that user.

In the default installation, the Teamwork Cloud service must run under the Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) user account. All related files must be owned by the twcloud user and must have full permissions (rwx) granted exclusively to this user. No other users are permitted to have any access to these files.

# ll jmx.* 
-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 20 Mar 16 15:11 jmx.access 
-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 26 Apr 21 10:41 jmx.password

The default settings for the JVM are located in <strong>java.security</strong>. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58d87dcd-e8d2-4242-a939-315d9bf88e79"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>We can check the ciphers/protocols being used by the applications using nmap (version 7.x) or TestSSLServer.jar, available from <a style="text-align: left;" href="https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x" class="" rel="nofollow">https://community.rsa.com/s/article/How-to-check-for-available-SSL-TLS-protocols-and-ciphers-for-a-specific-port-in-RSA-Authentication-Manager-8-x</a>.</p><p>As an example, below is a scan using both tools against a default installation. In this example, we will be testing port 8111, the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud </span>port.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="798c7a79-116d-4564-a5ed-dd4df3dbe396"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 8111 127.0.0.1
